title: Radiographic Imaging & Chest X-ray Interpretation Quiz

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the beam path and utility of a standard PA chest radiograph?

answers.text: X-rays travel from anterior to posterior, causing artificial magnification of the heart shadow
X-rays travel from posterior to anterior, minimizing artificial magnification of the heart silhouette
X-rays enter laterally to assess small pneumothoraces
X-rays are directed subglottically to evaluate upper airway obstruction

questions.text: An AP chest radiograph is commonly used in the ICU for bedridden patients. How does this projection affect the appearance of the heart?

answers.text: It artificially magnifies the heart shadow because the heart is further from the image receptor
It causes the heart shadow to appear smaller than its true anatomical size
It has no effect on the cardiac silhouette compared to a PA projection
It shifts the heart entirely into the right hemithorax

questions.text: Which radiographic projection is the most valuable for detecting a small pleural effusion?
A lateral neck radiograph of a child with upper airway obstruction reveals the classic 'thumb sign'. This finding is indicative of:
The 'steeple sign' seen on an upper airway radiograph represents subglottic narrowing characteristic of:
When assessing the technical quality of a chest X-ray for proper penetration, which landmark should be verified?

answers.text: The liver shadow should be entirely radiolucent
The thoracic vertebrae should be just visible behind the heart shadow
The lung parenchyma should appear completely black without any visible blood vessels
The intercostal spaces must be completely obliterated

questions.text: To verify that a chest radiograph was taken with adequate inspiration, how many ribs should be visible above the diaphragm?
On a normal chest radiograph, the right hemidiaphragm sits slightly higher than the left at the level of the sixth anterior rib. What anatomical structure accounts for this?
Blunting or obliteration of the costophrenic angles on a chest X-ray is a classic finding indicative of:
What is the normal expected cardiothoracic (C/T) ratio on a standard PA chest radiograph?
Where should the tip of a properly positioned Endotracheal Tube (ETT) be located on a chest radiograph?

answers.text: At the level of the vocal cords
Directly inside the right mainstem bronchus

questions.text: When verifying the placement of a Central Venous Catheter (CVC) on an X-ray, the catheter tip should reside in the:

answers.text: Lower portion of the superior vena cava, proximal to the right atrium

questions.text: Where should the tip of a properly positioned Pulmonary Artery (Swan-Ganz) catheter appear on a chest radiograph?
To prevent subcutaneous air leaks, what specific placement criteria applies to a chest tube seen on an X-ray?

answers.text: The tube must cross the midline into the contralateral hemithorax
The break in the radiopaque line must be fully inside the pleural space
The tip must terminate inside the superior vena cava
The drainage eyelets must remain external to the chest wall

questions.text: A nasogastric (NG) or feeding tube is confirmed to be in optimal position when its radiopaque tip is seen:

answers.text: In the stomach 2 – 6 cm below the diaphragm
In the left lower lobe bronchus
